title: Pros and Cons of Naive Bayes
concept: Naive Bayes is fast and handles small datasets well, but it suffers from the 'Zero Probability Problem' when encountering unseen features.

exercises.prompt: Recap: What is the 'bag of words' assumption in text classification?

options.text: Words are treated as independent items, ignoring grammar and word order
Words are arranged in alphabetical order before analysis
Words are converted into spatial coordinates for distance calculation

exercises.explain: Yes! The bag of words approach treats text as an unordered collection of words, counting how often each word occurs.

cards.title: The unseen clue
cards.body: Imagine a detective who has solved hundreds of cases. Suddenly, they find a clue they have never seen before, like a rare purple flower. Because they have zero record of this flower, their calculations get completely stuck. This is a classic challenge for Naive Bayes.

cards.title: Strengths and weaknesses
cards.body: Naive Bayes is fast to train, computationally cheap, and performs well even with small amounts of training data. However, its biggest weakness is the Zero Probability Problem: if a feature was never seen during training, its probability becomes exactly 0%.

cards.title: The math spoiler
cards.body: Remember how Naive Bayes multiplies probabilities together? In math, what happens when you multiply any number by zero? 5 × 0 = 0. If one word in an email has a 0% probability because it is brand new, the probability for the entire email instantly becomes 0%, completely breaking the prediction!

cards.title: A new word appears
cards.body: If a spam filter is trained on emails and then receives an email with the word "biodegradable" (which was never in the training set), the model says the probability of "biodegradable" is 0. Multiplying all the other word probabilities by 0 makes the final probability 0. The filter gets confused!

cards.title: Laplace Smoothing
cards.body: You might think a single unseen word ruins Naive Bayes forever. Luckily, developers use a simple trick called Laplace Smoothing. We add a tiny count (usually 1) to every single word in our vocabulary, ensuring no probability is ever exactly zero.

cards.title: Naive Bayes Trade-offs
cards.body: The balance sheet of the algorithm:

left.text:
• Incredibly fast
• Works with small data
• Easy to build and scale

right.text:
• Independent assumption is naive
• Zero Probability Problem on new data

exercises.prompt: Why does a single unseen feature or word pose a threat to a Naive Bayes model?

options.text: Because its calculated probability is 0%, and multiplying by 0 makes the final probability of the entire class 0%
Because it causes the model to run out of memory
Because it makes the model switch to a regression mode
Because it changes the labels of all other features

exercises.explain: Yes! Because Naive Bayes multiplies probabilities, a single zero ruins the entire product. This is the Zero Probability Problem.

exercises.prompt: True or false: Naive Bayes requires millions of examples to make decent predictions, making it bad for small datasets.
exercises.explain: False! Naive Bayes actually works surprisingly well with relatively small datasets compared to other complex models.

exercises.sentence: To prevent unseen features from turning the entire probability to zero, developers use a trick called Laplace ___.
exercises.explain: Laplace smoothing adds a small number (like 1) to all counts so no probability is ever exactly zero.

exercises.prompt: Which of the following is a classic advantage of Naive Bayes?

options.text: It can be trained extremely quickly and uses very little computing power
It understands complex grammar structures and slang perfectly
It automatically groups data without needing any labels

exercises.explain: Exactly! Naive Bayes is extremely fast to train and has very low computational overhead.

exercises.prompt: A new email contains the words 'Congratulations' and a brand-new scientific term 'cryo-preservation'. The model has never seen 'cryo-preservation' before. If Laplace Smoothing is NOT used, what happens to the calculated probability that the email is spam?

options.text: The probability becomes exactly 0, meaning the model cannot evaluate it correctly
The probability becomes 100% because of the word 'Congratulations'
The model ignores 'cryo-preservation' and only calculates the probability of 'Congratulations'
The model asks the user to manually define the new term first

exercises.explain: Correct! Without smoothing, the unseen word gets a probability of 0. When multiplied by the other probabilities, the final spam probability becomes 0, breaking the prediction. With smoothing, it would receive a tiny non-zero probability. Next, let's explore where we use Naive Bayes in the real world!
